summary:
The domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris) is the oldest known domesticated animal, with its divergence from wild wolf populations estimated by genetic and archaeological evidence to have occurred between approximately 15,000 and 40,000 years ago. However, by 10,000–9,000 BCE, dogs were clearly integrated into human societies across multiple regions, with archaeological evidence confirming their presence as working and companion animals in communities spanning western Europe, western Asia, and East Asia.

The exact geographic origin or origins of dog domestication remain debated. Genetic studies have proposed single-origin hypotheses — placing domestication in Central Asia, East Asia, or Europe at various times — as well as multi-regional models suggesting wolves were domesticated independently more than once. What is clear is that by the period under consideration, dogs were assisting humans in hunting, guarding settlements, and possibly herding, and were sometimes buried alongside their human companions — as documented at [[event:4|Natufian]] burials in the Levant. Dogs represent the earliest example of humans forming a sustained interspecies partnership that would later extend to sheep, goats, cattle, and other domesticated animals.
